text: The surgeon applies a clamp to the renal artery, visibly occluding the blood flow to the kidney in preparation for the partial nephrectomy.
The surgeon selectively clamps the vessel supplying the upper pole of the kidney, preparing to remove an abnormality located at the upper pole.
The surgeon identifies and occludes the feeding blood vessel to the kidney, resulting in a bloodless surgical field during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully peels away a small tumor from the kidney, placing it aside for retrieval at the end of the procedure. The tumor separates easily within the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis by suturing the base of the kidney using a barbed V-Lock suture with a large needle. The suture features a loop at the end, allowing it to be snugged down without tying a knot, before closing the defect.
The surgeon has restored blood flow to the upper pole of the kidney, which was previously selectively clamped, allowing the tissue to regain its normal coloration.
The surgical field shows minimal bleeding as the tumor is located at the very edge of the kidney, where the blood vessels are less numerous.
The surgeon runs a continuous suture up the length of the kidney base using a barbed suture, ensuring the stitch holds securely without slippage.

The surgeon uses a hemlock clip to snug the stitch down, then continues to run the stitch along the kidney's exterior. The barbs on the stitch visibly hold the kidney tissue together, allowing for tension adjustment with the hemlock clip.
The surgeon places the final clip using robotic assistance to complete the partial nephrectomy procedure.
